lead: This week, innovation takes center stage with a focus on AI advancements, startup breakthroughs, and the evolving landscape of wearable technology.

stories.title: McKinsey built a free AI tool so candidates stop paying $500/hour interview coaches
stories.summary: McKinsey has launched a free AI practice tool aimed at leveling the playing field for job candidates. This tool allows unlimited attempts at a quantitative case study, which is commonly faced in interviews for entry-level roles. It is designed to help those who cannot afford expensive coaching services, providing a significant resource for aspiring business analysts and associates.

stories.title: This Zurich startup built a four-armed robot for space stations. Each astronaut hour it saves is worth $140,000.
stories.summary: Orbit Robotics, a Zurich-based startup, has unveiled Helios, a four-armed robot designed for use in space stations. The robot's unique design replaces legs with two additional arms, allowing it to anchor itself while performing tasks in microgravity. This innovation aims to save astronaut hours, valued at $140,000 each, showcasing a significant advancement in space technology.

stories.title: The US blacklisted Anthropic as a security threat. Its spy agencies are using Claude anyway.
stories.summary: Despite being blacklisted by the US government due to security concerns, the NSA continues to utilize Anthropic's AI model, Claude. This situation highlights the complexities of national security and the reliance on advanced AI technologies, even when they pose potential risks. The Pentagon's decision underscores the critical need for AI solutions in intelligence operations.

stories.title: CBA's DevOps agent is helping on-call engineers on 2am wake-up duty
stories.summary: Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA) has developed a DevOps agent that assists on-call engineers during late-night incidents. This tool helps identify root causes of issues, reducing the burden on engineers who are often called in during off-hours. The initiative reflects a growing trend in leveraging technology to improve operational efficiency in the banking sector.

stories.title: Xreal, Google’s smartglasses partner, thinks it has finally mastered this notoriously tricky industry
stories.summary: Xreal, in partnership with Google, believes it has finally cracked the code in the smart glasses market, which has historically struggled with consumer adoption. The company aims to integrate augmented reality seamlessly into daily life, marking a potential turning point for wearable technology. This development could reshape how users interact with their environments.

stories.title: On London's streets, facial recognition tests the balance between security and liberty
stories.summary: The UK government is exploring the implications of facial recognition technology in public spaces, aiming to establish a legal framework that balances security needs with individual liberties. This initiative raises important questions about privacy and surveillance in modern society, as cities increasingly adopt advanced technologies for safety.

stories.title: The Apple Watch is 11 years old and losing momentum. Screenless rivals are winning the next phase.
stories.summary: As the Apple Watch celebrates its 11th anniversary, it faces increasing competition from screenless wearables that cater to evolving consumer preferences. The original smartwatch has generated significant sales but is now at risk of losing its market lead as innovation slows. This shift highlights the dynamic nature of the wearable technology landscape.

stories.title: Employers Mutual Limited strengthens cyber defences with XDR shift
stories.summary: Employers Mutual Limited has enhanced its cybersecurity measures by transitioning to Extended Detection and Response (XDR) systems. This move aims to bolster client data protection and address the growing threat of cyber attacks. The shift reflects a broader trend among organizations to adopt advanced security technologies in response to increasing digital risks.

stories.title: The Gulf was supposed to power the AI boom. Then the war started.
stories.summary: The ongoing conflict in the Middle East has put a strain on the Gulf's ambitions to become a global AI hub, particularly following attacks on data centers. As oil prices soar and regional stability falters, the future of AI development in the area faces significant challenges. This situation underscores the intersection of geopolitics and technology.
